---
title: "AI Basics with AK"
subtitle: "Season 03 - Introduction to Statistics"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: false
    auto-stretch: true
    slide-number: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Episode 10 - Hypothesis Testing

## Recap: What We've Covered So Far

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
| Episode | Topic                 | Key Takeaway                              |
|----------------------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| **07**  | Central Limit Theorem | Sample means become Normal as n grows     |
| **08**  | Confidence Intervals  | A range of plausible values for the truth |
| **09**  | T-Distribution        | When σ is unknown, tails get heavier      |

This episode ties everything together.

> We've been estimating. Now we ask: **Can we make a decision?**
:::

## The Human Problem Behind Hypothesis Testing

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### A Story We All Relate To

::: {style="font-size: 55%;"}
A company claims their new drug reduces blood pressure.

You test it on 40 patients.

The average blood pressure **did drop**.

But wait — is that because the drug worked?

Or just... random chance?

**How do you decide?**
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### This Is Exactly What Hypothesis Testing Solves

::: {style="font-size: 55%;"}
-   We observe something in a sample
-   We ask: *"Could this have happened by chance?"*
-   We use math to answer that question

This is the backbone of:

-   Medical trials 💊
-   A/B testing 🖥️
-   Quality control 🏭
-   Policy decisions 🏛️
:::
::::
:::::::

## Setting Up the Question

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
Before running any test, we must define **two competing claims**:

> **Null Hypothesis (H₀):** The "boring" claim. Nothing happened. No effect. No difference.

> **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁ or Hₐ):** The "interesting" claim. Something changed. There is an effect.

**The game:**

We start by *assuming H₀ is true* — and then ask:

*"If H₀ were true, how surprising is what we observed?"*

If it's surprising enough → we reject H₀.
:::

## Real World Examples

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
| Situation | H₀ (Null) | H₁ (Alternative) |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------------------|
| Drug trial | Drug has no effect | Drug reduces blood pressure |
| Website A/B test | Both versions perform equally | Version B gets more clicks |
| Coin fairness | Coin is fair (p = 0.5) | Coin is biased (p ≠ 0.5) |
| Manufacturing | Machine produces 500g on average | Machine is off — mean ≠ 500g |

**Notice:** H₀ always represents the *status quo* or *no change*.
:::

## The Core Logic: Innocent Until Proven Guilty

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Think of It Like a Trial

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
-   Defendant = H₀
-   Evidence = your data
-   Verdict = reject or fail to reject H₀

**We never say H₀ is "proven true."**

We either:

-   Reject H₀ (evidence is strong enough)
-   Fail to reject H₀ (not enough evidence)
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Important Mindset Shift

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
❌ We do NOT prove H₁ is true

❌ We do NOT prove H₀ is false

✅ We measure how *inconsistent* the data is with H₀

> The strength of evidence is what drives the conclusion.
:::
::::
:::::::

## Introducing the p-value

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
The **p-value** is the probability of seeing results *as extreme or more extreme* than what we observed — **assuming H₀ is true.**

> "If the null hypothesis were true, how often would we see data like this?"

-   **Small p-value** → Our data is very unlikely under H₀ → Evidence against H₀
-   **Large p-value** → Our data is quite plausible under H₀ → No strong evidence against H₀

**The threshold (significance level α):**

Most commonly α = 0.05

If p-value \< α → Reject H₀
:::

## Visualizing the p-value

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy import stats

np.random.seed(42)

# Setup
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 500)
y = stats.norm.pdf(x)

# Observed test statistic
test_stats = [1.2, 2.0, 2.8]
labels = ["Weak evidence (z=1.2)", "Moderate evidence (z=2.0)", "Strong evidence (z=2.8)"]
colors = ["#f0a500", "#e05c00", "#cc0000"]

fig = go.Figure()

# Base normal curve - always visible
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=x, y=y,
    mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='steelblue', width=2.5),
    name='Null Distribution (H₀)',
    visible=True
))

for i, (z, label, color) in enumerate(zip(test_stats, labels, colors)):
    visible = (i == 0)
    
    # Right tail fill
    x_tail = x[x >= z]
    y_tail = stats.norm.pdf(x_tail)
    pval = round(2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z))), 4)  # two-tailed

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=np.concatenate([[x_tail[0]], x_tail, [x_tail[-1]]]),
        y=np.concatenate([[0], y_tail, [0]]),
        fill='toself',
        fillcolor=color,
        opacity=0.4,
        line=dict(color=color),
        name=f'Right tail (p/2)',
        visible=visible,
        showlegend=False
    ))

    # Left tail fill
    x_left = x[x <= -z]
    y_left = stats.norm.pdf(x_left)

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=np.concatenate([[x_left[0]], x_left, [x_left[-1]]]),
        y=np.concatenate([[0], y_left, [0]]),
        fill='toself',
        fillcolor=color,
        opacity=0.4,
        line=dict(color=color),
        name=f'Left tail (p/2)',
        visible=visible,
        showlegend=False
    ))

    # Vertical line at test stat
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[z, z],
        y=[0, stats.norm.pdf(z)],
        mode='lines',
        line=dict(color=color, dash='dash', width=2),
        name=f'z = {z}',
        visible=visible
    ))

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[-z, -z],
        y=[0, stats.norm.pdf(-z)],
        mode='lines',
        line=dict(color=color, dash='dash', width=2),
        name=f'z = -{z}',
        visible=visible,
        showlegend=False
    ))

# Dropdown buttons
n_traces_per = 4  # tail_right, tail_left, vline_right, vline_left
base = 1  # after the base normal curve

buttons = []
for i, (z, label, color) in enumerate(zip(test_stats, labels, colors)):
    vis = [True]  # base curve always visible
    for j in range(3):
        for k in range(n_traces_per):
            vis.append(j == i)
    pval = round(2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z))), 4)
    buttons.append(dict(
        label=label,
        method='update',
        args=[
            {'visible': vis},
            {'title': f'p-value Visualization | z = {z} | p-value = {pval} (two-tailed)'}
        ]
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title='p-value Visualization | z = 1.2 | p-value = 0.2301 (two-tailed)',
    xaxis_title='Test Statistic (z)',
    yaxis_title='Probability Density',
    height=480,
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons,
        direction='down',
        x=0.01,
        y=1.18,
        xanchor='left',
        yanchor='top'
    )],
    showlegend=True
)

fig.show()
```

::: {style="font-size: 50%;"}
> The shaded area is the p-value. Smaller shaded area = stronger evidence against H₀.
:::

## The 5-Step Framework for Hypothesis Testing

::: {style="font-size: 68%;"}
Every hypothesis test follows the same recipe:

**Step 1 — State H₀ and H₁** Define your null and alternative hypotheses clearly.

**Step 2 — Choose significance level α** Usually 0.05. This is your "how surprised must I be?" threshold.

**Step 3 — Compute the test statistic** Standardize your observed result (z-score or t-score).

**Step 4 — Find the p-value** How likely is your result if H₀ were true?

**Step 5 — Make a decision** p-value \< α → Reject H₀ p-value ≥ α → Fail to reject H₀
:::

## One-Tailed vs Two-Tailed Tests

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Two-Tailed Test

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
Use when H₁ says: *"different from"*

Example: \> H₀: μ = 500g \> H₁: μ **≠** 500g

You care if the machine is **over or under** producing.

Critical region is split — both tails.

α = 0.05 → each tail gets 0.025
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### One-Tailed Test

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
Use when H₁ says: *"greater than"* or *"less than"*

Example: \> H₀: μ = 500g \> H₁: μ **\>** 500g

You only care if it's producing **too much**.

Critical region is on one side only.

α = 0.05 → entire 0.05 goes to one tail
:::
::::
:::::::

## Visualizing One-Tailed vs Two-Tailed

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy import stats

x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 500)
y = stats.norm.pdf(x)
alpha = 0.05

# Critical values
z_two = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)   # 1.96
z_one_right = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha)  # 1.645

fig = go.Figure()

# Normal curve
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=x, y=y, mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='steelblue', width=2.5),
    name='Null Distribution'
))

# Two-tailed: both tails
for sign, label in [(-1, 'Left rejection zone'), (1, 'Right rejection zone')]:
    x_tail = x[x * sign >= z_two] if sign == 1 else x[x <= -z_two]
    y_tail = stats.norm.pdf(x_tail)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=np.concatenate([[x_tail[0]], x_tail, [x_tail[-1]]]),
        y=np.concatenate([[0], y_tail, [0]]),
        fill='toself', fillcolor='rgba(220, 50, 50, 0.4)',
        line=dict(color='rgba(220,50,50,0)'),
        name=label, visible=True
    ))

# One-tailed: right tail only
x_right = x[x >= z_one_right]
y_right = stats.norm.pdf(x_right)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=np.concatenate([[x_right[0]], x_right, [x_right[-1]]]),
    y=np.concatenate([[0], y_right, [0]]),
    fill='toself', fillcolor='rgba(50, 180, 50, 0.4)',
    line=dict(color='rgba(50,180,50,0)'),
    name='Right rejection zone (one-tailed)', visible=False
))

buttons = [
    dict(
        label='Two-Tailed Test (H₁: μ ≠ μ₀)',
        method='update',
        args=[{'visible': [True, True, True, False]},
              {'title': 'Two-Tailed Test | α = 0.05 | Critical values: ±1.96'}]
    ),
    dict(
        label='One-Tailed Test (H₁: μ > μ₀)',
        method='update',
        args=[{'visible': [True, False, False, True]},
              {'title': 'One-Tailed Test (Right) | α = 0.05 | Critical value: 1.645'}]
    )
]

fig.update_layout(
    title='Two-Tailed Test | α = 0.05 | Critical values: ±1.96',
    xaxis_title='Test Statistic',
    yaxis_title='Probability Density',
    height=450,
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons, direction='down',
        x=0.01, y=1.18,
        xanchor='left', yanchor='top'
    )],
    showlegend=True
)

fig.show()
```

## Worked Example: Coin Fairness Test

::: {style="font-size: 68%;"}
**The Setup:** You suspect a coin is biased. You flip it 100 times and get **62 heads**.

Is the coin fair?

**Step 1:** H₀: p = 0.5 \| H₁: p ≠ 0.5 (two-tailed)

**Step 2:** α = 0.05

**Step 3:** Compute z-score:

$$z = \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}} = \frac{0.62 - 0.5}{\sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{100}}} = \frac{0.12}{0.05} = 2.4$$

**Step 4:** p-value = 2 × P(Z \> 2.4) ≈ 0.016

**Step 5:** 0.016 \< 0.05 → **Reject H₀**

Conclusion: Evidence suggests the coin is biased.
:::

## Let's See It Interactively

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy import stats

np.random.seed(42)

# Simulate coin flip experiments
n_flips = 100
p_true = 0.5
n_simulations = 1000

results = []
for heads in [45, 52, 62, 70]:
    p_hat = heads / n_flips
    z = (p_hat - p_true) / np.sqrt(p_true * (1 - p_true) / n_flips)
    p_val = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z)))
    decision = "Reject H₀ ❌" if p_val < 0.05 else "Fail to Reject H₀ ✅"
    results.append((heads, p_hat, round(z, 3), round(p_val, 4), decision))

x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 500)
y_norm = stats.norm.pdf(x)

fig = go.Figure()

# Base curve always visible
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=x, y=y_norm, mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='steelblue', width=2.5),
    name='Null Distribution'
))

# Critical region (alpha=0.05, two-tailed)
for sign in [1, -1]:
    x_crit = x[x * sign >= 1.96] if sign == 1 else x[x <= -1.96]
    y_crit = stats.norm.pdf(x_crit)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=np.concatenate([[x_crit[0]], x_crit, [x_crit[-1]]]),
        y=np.concatenate([[0], y_crit, [0]]),
        fill='toself', fillcolor='rgba(220,50,50,0.2)',
        line=dict(color='rgba(220,50,50,0)'),
        name='Rejection region (α=0.05)',
        showlegend=(sign == 1)
    ))

# Per scenario vertical lines (hidden initially, shown via dropdown)
for i, (heads, p_hat, z_val, p_val, decision) in enumerate(results):
    vis = (i == 0)
    color = '#cc0000' if 'Reject' in decision else '#228B22'
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[z_val, z_val], y=[0, stats.norm.pdf(z_val)],
        mode='lines+text',
        line=dict(color=color, width=2.5, dash='dash'),
        text=['', f'z={z_val}'],
        textposition='top center',
        name=f'{heads} heads → {decision}',
        visible=vis
    ))

# Build dropdown
buttons = []
for i, (heads, p_hat, z_val, p_val, decision) in enumerate(results):
    vis = [True, True, True]  # base curve + 2 critical regions
    for j in range(len(results)):
        vis.append(j == i)
    buttons.append(dict(
        label=f'{heads} heads out of 100',
        method='update',
        args=[
            {'visible': vis},
            {'title': f'{heads} heads | z = {z_val} | p-value = {p_val} | {decision}'}
        ]
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title=f'{results[0][0]} heads | z = {results[0][2]} | p-value = {results[0][3]} | {results[0][4]}',
    xaxis_title='Test Statistic (z)',
    yaxis_title='Probability Density',
    height=470,
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons, direction='down',
        x=0.01, y=1.18,
        xanchor='left', yanchor='top'
    )],
    showlegend=True
)

fig.show()
```

::: {style="font-size: 50%;"}
> Select different coin flip outcomes to see how the evidence changes.
:::

# Thank You
